context: Broad selling today — most large-caps fell. Down days are exactly when disciplined SIP investors keep buying units cheaper.
Money rotated into FMCG and out of Realty. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (13.29) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.teaser: Listed in the Constitution's Eighth Schedule — Hindi isn't the 'national' language.
gk.more: Hindi is an official language of the Union alongside English; no language is legally 'national'. The 22 include Bengali, Telugu, Marathi, Tamil, Urdu, Gujarati and Sanskrit. India has 120+ languages with 10,000+ speakers each — the world's most multilingual large country.

gk.t: Why the sea is salty but rivers are not
gk.teaser: Rivers carry tiny amounts of salt to the sea — and the sea never drains.
gk.more: Rainwater dissolves minute quantities of minerals from rocks into rivers. Rivers flow to the sea, where water evaporates but salt stays behind, concentrating over billions of years. Rivers taste fresh only because their salt keeps moving on instead of accumulating.

gk.t: ISRO launched 104 satellites in one rocket
gk.teaser: A world record in 2017, from a single PSLV flight.
gk.more: Most were tiny foreign 'nanosatellites' — proof of ISRO's reputation as the world's budget-friendly, reliable launcher. India was also first to reach Mars orbit on its first attempt (Mangalyaan, 2014) — at a cost less than the movie Gravity.

gk.t: India's Constitution is the world's longest
gk.more: B.R. Ambedkar chaired the drafting committee. It borrowed deliberately: parliamentary system from Britain, fundamental rights from the US, directive principles from Ireland, emergency provisions from Germany. Jan 26 was chosen because Congress declared 'Purna Swaraj' on that date in 1930.

gk.teaser: India's central bank predates independence by 12 years.
gk.more: Set up under the RBI Act 1934 based on the Hilton Young Commission, it was privately owned until nationalisation in 1949. It issues currency, sets the repo rate, regulates banks, and manages the rupee — its first governor was Osborne Smith; C.D. Deshmukh was the first Indian governor.

With forex reserves down to weeks of imports, PM Narasimha Rao and FM Manmohan Singh dismantled the licence raj, devalued the rupee, cut tariffs and opened doors to foreign investment. Nearly every big private-sector story since — IT, telecom, startups — traces to this pivot.

gk.teaser: Land heats faster than sea — wind follows.
gk.more: By June the subcontinent's landmass is much hotter than the Indian Ocean, creating a low-pressure zone that pulls moisture-laden winds northeast. They break on the Western Ghats and Himalayas as rain. About 70% of India's annual rainfall arrives in these four months, which is why a 'bad monsoon' moves inflation, farm incomes and even elections.

gk.t: Ashoka: the emperor who renounced war
gk.teaser: His wheel sits at the centre of the tricolour.
gk.more: After the bloody Kalinga war (~261 BCE), Mauryan emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism and spread edicts on rock pillars across the subcontinent. The Ashoka Chakra on India's flag and the Lion Capital national emblem both come from his pillars at Sarnath.

WWI started with an assassination in Sarajevo and ended empires (Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian Tsar). WWII began with Germany invading Poland and ended with atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Over 2.5 million Indian soldiers served in WWII — the largest volunteer army in history.

gk.teaser: Founded 1945 by 51 countries so WWIII never happens.
gk.more: Headquartered in New York, its Security Council has five veto-holding permanent members: US, UK, France, Russia, China. India is a founding member and the largest cumulative contributor of UN peacekeeping troops, but its long push for a permanent Security Council seat remains unresolved.

gkMore.t: The world's first stock exchange opened in Amsterdam
gkMore.teaser: The Dutch East India Company sold the first public shares.
gkMore.more: In 1602 the Dutch East India Company (VOC) became the first firm to issue tradable shares to the public, and the Amsterdam exchange grew up around trading them. It pioneered ideas we still use daily — shares, dividends and speculation. It also witnessed some of the earliest recorded short selling and market bubbles.

gkMore.t: History's worst inflation doubled prices every 15 hours
gkMore.more: In mid-1946 Hungary's pengő suffered the most extreme hyperinflation ever recorded, with prices roughly doubling every 15 hours. The state issued a banknote worth 100 quintillion pengő, the highest denomination ever put into circulation. The currency was scrapped and replaced by the forint, which Hungary still uses today.

gkMore.teaser: When its west wakes, its far east is already asleep.
gkMore.more: Russia is so wide that it covers 11 time zones — more than any other single continuous stretch of land. When it is morning in Kaliningrad in the west, it is already evening in Kamchatka on the Pacific coast. France, counting its scattered overseas territories, technically touches even more zones, but Russia's are all contiguous.

gkMore.t: The Dead Sea is Earth's lowest exposed land
gkMore.teaser: Its shore sits over 400 metres below sea level.
gkMore.more: The surface of the Dead Sea lies more than 430 metres below sea level, the lowest dry land on the planet, and it keeps dropping roughly a metre a year. It is nearly ten times saltier than the ocean — so dense that swimmers float effortlessly. Almost nothing lives in it, which is how it earned its name.

gkMore.teaser: The Indian plate keeps pushing it higher.
gkMore.more: The Himalayas rose when the Indian tectonic plate collided with Asia, and that collision is still underway. Everest gains a few millimetres each year as the plates keep pressing together. Earthquakes can nudge its height up or down, one reason it has been remeasured over time — now officially about 8,849 metres.

gkMore.t: The first computer 'bug' was a real insect
gkMore.teaser: A moth got trapped in a 1947 machine.
gkMore.more: In 1947, operators of the Harvard Mark II computer found a moth stuck in a relay, causing a malfunction. They taped it into the logbook with the note 'first actual case of bug being found.' The pioneering programmer Grace Hopper helped popularise the tale, cementing 'debugging' in the language — though engineers had loosely used 'bug' for faults even earlier.

gkMore.t: Computing power has doubled roughly every two years
gkMore.teaser: Moore's Law shaped the entire digital age.
gkMore.more: In 1965 Intel co-founder Gordon Moore observed that the number of transistors on a chip was doubling about every year; he revised the pace to roughly every two years in 1975, and the industry turned that observation into a roadmap. This relentless doubling is why today's smartphone vastly outmuscles the computers that guided Apollo 11 to the Moon. The pace is finally slowing as transistors shrink toward the size of a few atoms.

In October 1969, researchers tried to send the word 'LOGIN' between two computers on ARPANET, the internet's ancestor. The network crashed after just two letters, so the first message ever transmitted was 'lo.' They fixed it within the hour, and the full link soon worked — the humble start of a technology that now connects billions.

gkMore.t: Bluetooth is named after a Viking king
gkMore.teaser: And its logo secretly spells his initials.
gkMore.more: Bluetooth is named after Harald 'Bluetooth' Gormsson, a 10th-century Danish king who united warring tribes — just as the technology unites different devices. Its logo combines the Younger Futhark runes for his initials, H and B. The king's nickname supposedly came from a dead, bluish-grey tooth.

gkMore.t: Five countries can veto any UN Security Council decision
gkMore.teaser: A single 'no' can block the world.
gkMore.more: The UN Security Council has five permanent members — the US, UK, France, Russia and China — each able to veto any substantive resolution. This structure, fixed after World War II, means one great power can halt collective action. India, Japan, Germany and Brazil have long campaigned for permanent seats, so far without success.

learn.title: GDP: the number that rules economics — and its blind spots
body: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total market value of all goods and services produced within a country over a given period, usually a year or a quarter. It is the headline measure of an economy's size, and its growth rate is watched closely by governments, investors and voters. India is among the world's largest economies by total GDP, though its GDP per person — the total divided by population — remains far lower than that of rich nations.

The trouble is that GDP was never designed to measure how well people are actually living. It counts activity regardless of whether it improves lives: cleaning up an oil spill or rebuilding after a disaster adds to GDP, while unpaid housework and caregiving, which have enormous real value, are left out entirely. It also says nothing about how income is distributed, so a country can post strong growth while most of the gains flow to a small elite.

Because of these blind spots, economists supplement GDP with other measures. The Human Development Index blends income with health and education; other indicators try to account for inequality or environmental damage. GDP remains indispensable as a quick gauge of economic size and momentum, but a curious observer should treat it as one dashboard dial among many, not the whole picture.

learn.title: How central banks steer an economy with one main lever
learn.teaser: The interest rate is the economy's thermostat.
body: A central bank — the Reserve Bank of India at home, the Federal Reserve in the US — is the institution that manages a nation's money. Its most powerful tool is the benchmark interest rate, in India called the repo rate, which is the rate at which it lends to commercial banks. Changing this rate ripples out to the interest on home loans, business credit and savings across the whole economy.

The logic is like a thermostat. When inflation runs too hot, the central bank raises rates, making borrowing costlier, cooling spending and investment, and easing price pressure. When the economy is sluggish, it cuts rates to make credit cheap and encourage activity. Central banks also manage the money supply, hold the country's foreign-exchange reserves, oversee the banking system, and act as a 'lender of last resort' during financial panics.

This power comes with hard trade-offs. Fighting inflation with high rates can slow growth and raise unemployment, so central bankers are constantly balancing competing risks. That is also why their independence from short-term political pressure is prized: governments facing elections are tempted to keep money cheap, but a credible, independent central bank can make the unpopular choices needed to keep prices stable over the long run.

learn.title: Why starting to invest early beats investing a lot
learn.teaser: Time is the most powerful force in finance.
body: Compound interest is often called the eighth wonder of the world, and for good reason. When you invest, you earn returns not just on your original money but also on all the returns it has already generated. Over short periods this looks unremarkable, but over decades it snowballs dramatically — the growth curve bends sharply upward as the gains start generating their own gains.

This is why time matters more than the size of your contributions. Someone who invests a modest amount every month starting in their twenties can end up with far more than someone who invests much larger sums but only begins in their forties, simply because the early money had more years to compound. In India, Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) into equity mutual funds are a popular way to harness this, though what really matters is investing regularly and staying invested through the ups and downs.

The same force works in reverse and can quietly ruin people. High-interest debt — credit-card balances, some personal loans — compounds against you just as relentlessly, so an unpaid balance can balloon. The practical takeaways are simple but powerful: start early, keep costs and needless debt low, focus on real returns after inflation, and let time do the heavy lifting.

learn.title: How governments borrow — and why bond yields matter
learn.teaser: A government IOU that moves the whole economy.
body: Governments almost always spend more than they collect in taxes, and they cover the gap by borrowing — mainly by issuing bonds. A bond is essentially an IOU: investors lend the government money today in exchange for regular interest payments and the return of their principal on a fixed future date. Governments are usually seen as safe borrowers, so their bonds set a baseline 'risk-free' rate against which almost everything else in finance is priced.

The key number is the yield — the effective annual return a buyer earns. Yields rise when investors demand more compensation, for instance if they expect higher inflation or doubt the government's ability to repay, and they fall when investors seek safety. Crucially, bond prices and yields move in opposite directions: when demand pushes a bond's price up, its yield falls. Because central banks also buy and sell government bonds to influence the money supply, this market sits at the heart of the financial system.

For a country like India, these dynamics shape everyday life. The government's fiscal deficit — how much it borrows each year — affects how much credit is left for private businesses and what interest rates households pay. Credit-rating agencies judge the risk of sovereign debt, and their verdicts can raise or lower a nation's borrowing costs. Watching the 10-year government bond yield is one of the quickest ways to read the market's mood about an economy's future.

learn.title: How the US dollar became the world's money
learn.teaser: The quiet source of American power.
body: In July 1944, with World War II still raging, delegates from 44 nations met at Bretton Woods in the United States to design the postwar financial order. They agreed to peg their currencies to the US dollar, which alone remained convertible to gold at a fixed price. The US, holding most of the world's gold and its strongest economy, became the anchor of the global system, and institutions like the International Monetary Fund and World Bank were born there.

In 1971, facing pressure on its gold reserves, the US ended the dollar's convertibility to gold, and the fixed-rate system collapsed into today's world of floating currencies. Yet the dollar did not lose its crown. It remained the currency in which oil and most commodities were priced, the main reserve that central banks hold, and the default medium for international trade and finance — a position reinforced by the depth and reliability of American markets.

This 'exorbitant privilege' gives the United States extraordinary leverage: it can borrow cheaply, run large deficits, and impose financial sanctions that ripple worldwide, because so much global activity flows through the dollar. Rival powers, including China and members of the BRICS grouping, have sought to reduce this dependence, but no clear alternative has yet emerged at anything near the same scale.

learn.title: 1947: independence, Partition, and a subcontinent redrawn
body: At the stroke of midnight on 14–15 August 1947, British India won independence — but it was simultaneously split into two nations, India and Pakistan. The decision to partition along broadly religious lines had been taken hastily amid rising communal tension and British eagerness to leave. A British lawyer, Cyril Radcliffe, who had never before visited India, was given just weeks to draw the boundary line dividing Punjab and Bengal.

What followed was one of the largest and most violent human migrations in history. Some 15 million people crossed the new borders in both directions, Hindus and Sikhs moving into India and Muslims into Pakistan, often in terror. Estimates of the dead from the accompanying communal violence range from several hundred thousand to well over a million, with countless women abducted and families torn apart.

The legacy still shapes South Asia today. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel led the enormous task of integrating hundreds of princely states into the new India, but the fate of Jammu and Kashmir was left unresolved, sowing a dispute that has triggered wars and endures to this day. Partition remains a defining trauma and reference point in the politics of India, Pakistan and, after 1971, Bangladesh.

learn.title: The Cold War: how two superpowers divided the world
learn.teaser: Decades of rivalry without direct battle.
body: After World War II, the world split into two hostile camps: the capitalist West led by the United States, and the communist bloc led by the Soviet Union. From roughly 1947 to 1991, these superpowers waged a 'cold' war — a rivalry fought not through direct combat between them, which nuclear weapons made unthinkable, but through an arms race, espionage, propaganda, and a race to the Moon. The threat of mutual annihilation loomed constantly, coming closest to reality in the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis.

Instead of fighting each other head-on, the superpowers backed opposing sides in 'proxy' conflicts across the globe, from Korea and Vietnam to Afghanistan. Newly independent nations were pressured to pick a side. India, under Jawaharlal Nehru, helped lead the Non-Aligned Movement, seeking to chart an independent path and give the decolonising world a collective voice apart from either bloc.

The Cold War ended when the Soviet system, strained economically and politically, collapsed — symbolised by the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 and the dissolution of the USSR in 1991. That ushered in a brief 'unipolar' moment of American dominance. Today's world is increasingly described as multipolar, with the rise of China and the return of great-power competition echoing, in new forms, the old contest.

learn.title: Plate tectonics: why continents move and mountains rise
learn.teaser: The ground beneath you is slowly drifting.
body: Earth's rigid outer shell is not a single solid layer but a jigsaw of about a dozen large plates that float on the hot, slowly flowing rock of the mantle beneath. These plates creep across the surface at roughly the speed your fingernails grow — a few centimetres a year. Over millions of years, that imperceptible motion rearranges the entire face of the planet, opening oceans and raising mountain ranges.

What happens at plate boundaries shapes the world's geology. Where plates collide, crust is crumpled upward into mountains; where they pull apart, molten rock rises to form new seafloor; and where they grind past each other, stress builds and releases as earthquakes. The Pacific 'Ring of Fire,' a belt of volcanoes and quakes, traces exactly such boundaries.

India offers one of the most dramatic examples. The Indian landmass was once part of a southern supercontinent, Gondwana, and drifted thousands of kilometres north before slamming into Asia around 50 million years ago. That collision pushed up the Himalayas — and because India is still pressing northward, the mountains continue to rise and the region remains seismically active.
